observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review and reclassify the Loans Payable account in accordance with Section 80 of IPSAS 1 – Presentation of Financial Statements, by properly segregating the current and non-current portions based on the loan amortization schedules. Specifically, only the portion of the loan due within twelve months from the reporting date should be classified under Current Liabilities, while the remaining balance must be presented under Non-Current Liabilities.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the records per books with the results of the bank confirmation and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts to present the correct balance of the loans payable account in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer immediately investigate and account for the difference, and reconcile their records to reflect the accurate cash balance as of year-end.

We further recommended and Management agreed that going forward, at least quarterly reconciliations be strictly implemented to detect and correct any discrepancies in a timely manner, thereby ensuring the accuracy and reliability of financial reporting.

We reiterated our recommendation and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant pursue the immediate liquidation of all outstanding cash advances by issuing final demand letters to the accountable officers concerned. In cases where liquidation is still not effected within the prescribed period despite due notice, the appropriate sanctions should be enforced, including the withholding of salaries in accordance with existing rules and regulations.

Furthermore, we recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant conduct regular monitoring and timely reporting of unliquidated cash advances to the Local Chief Executive, to ensure proper oversight and to facilitate appropriate administrative actions as necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the difference amounting to ₱90,418,239.18 between the Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivables account balances.

To prevent the further accumulation of unreconciled differences, we further recommended that they, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, conduct regular and timely reconciliations of the total collectibles from the Basic and Special Education Taxes against the recorded RPT and SET Receivables account balances.

Additionally, to promote transparency and ensure full disclosure, we recommended that any remaining unreconciled differences be properly reported in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
a) The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile the
difference between the RPT and SET Receivables account balances
totaling ₱99,795,415.38 and henceforth, conduct periodic
reconciliation, together with the Municipal Assessor, of the total
collectibles from the Basic and Special Education Tax with the balances
of the RPT Receivables and SET Receivables accounts to avoid
accumulation of unreconciled differences;
b) The Municipal Treasurer maintain RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s
index card and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list
of taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for the current year
as basis for recording the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable
accounts in compliance with Section 20 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs,
Volume I; and
c) The Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers for accounts
RPT Receivable and SET Receivable in accordance with Section 10 of
the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended and Management agreed that they instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Draw a journal entry voucher (JEV) to adjust the Loans Payable and
Loans Receivable accounts;
b. Immediately book up the borrowing costs totaling ₱5,926,349.90
incurred from CY 2019 to CY 2021 by debiting CIP – Buildings and
Other Structures and crediting Prior Period Adjustment;
c. Determine the borrowing costs related to the construction of qualifying
assets from loans granted in prior years and make the necessary
adjustments to include the amount as part of the cost of the
infrastructure project.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer enforce collection of
delinquent real property taxes by conducting intensive tax campaign in every
barangay and by sending Notices of Delinquencies to all delinquent taxpayers.
Also, proceed to post the list of delinquent taxpayers in the conspicuous places of
the barangay halls. We further recommended that the Municipality avail of the
remedies in collecting delinquent real property taxes provided in Sections 256 to
260 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accounting
Office adjust the balances related to the PPE account as a change in accounting
policy and, subsequently, to record depreciation based on a 5% residual value in
compliance with PAG 6 of IPSAS 17. We also recommended that subsidiary
ledgers be maintained for all PPE accounts to ensure the accuracy and reliability
of their recorded book value.

We recommended and Management agreed that they:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher
to adjust the accounts affected. Moreover, withholding of taxes
should be in accordance with the provisions Section 4 of BIR
Revenue Regulation No. 12-2001 dated September 7, 2001;
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to require the contractors to utilize
the format prescribed under Department of Public Works and
Highways Order No. 46 series of 2022 dated March 23, 2022 in the
preparation of the SWA.
We further recommended that for succeeding payments related to
infrastructure projects, strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9184,
specifically the deduction of the recoupment of the advance payment from
the payments of progress billing to contractors; and consequently, use
appropriate accounts in the recording of payments related to infrastructure
projects.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer and
the disbursing officer to avoid using the cash advance for petty operating expenses
to pay for regular and recurring expenses.
We also recommended that Management establish reasonable estimate of
petty operating expenses to lessen the amount of cash handled by the disbursing
officer in order to prevent misappropriation thereof.
Lastly, we also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the grant and liquidation of
cash advances be duly supported with complete documentary requirements to
properly establish the validity of the transactions.

We recommended that:
a. The Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator, evaluate the feasibility,
and closely monitor the implementation of, the different PPAs of
the Municipality;
b. Management consider implementing some of the projects by
contract; and
c. The BAC Chairperson ensure the timely delivery of materials by
evaluating the competence of suppliers, charging for liquidated
damages where applicable, and blacklisting suppliers as necessary
and in accordance with the guidelines enumerated in Appendix 17
of the Updated 2016 Implementing Rules and Regulations of
Republic Act No. 9184 otherwise known as the Government
Procurement Reform Act.
We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide complete
and accurate information on the Quarterly Report on the Status of PPAs to ensure
their proper monitoring in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1.

We also recommended that Management effect the increase of the Fidelity
Bond of the DOs commensurate with their respective accountabilities, in
accordance with the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to
provide adequate protection of the Municipality’s resources.
Lastly, we recommended that Management avoid the granting of
additional CAs to the DOs unless their previous CAs have already been liquidated
in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be instructed to fast-
track the preparation of the financial statements and submit them to our office at
the soonest possible time.
We further recommended that Management closely monitor the strict
adherence to the requirements of pertinent laws and regulations relating to the
submission of financial statements and other accounting reports, and cause the
suspension of payment of salaries of employees responsible for the delayed
submission or non-submission until the same shall be complied.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief
Executive direct all department heads concerned through the issuance of a memo
to submit their respective duly accomplished AAPSI within a specific time frame
and assign a focal person to consolidate the same for submission to the Auditor
at the soonest possible time, not later than 60 days upon receipt of the AARs for
the succeeding years.
We further recommended and Management agreed that pertinent
documents to support the Actions Taken should also be submitted to facilitate
validation of the responses.

The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile the accounting records, and make the necessary adjustments so that the accounts can be fairly presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
a. Management create an inventory committee to conduct the physical
count of inventories and properties, and that RPCI and RPCPPE be
submitted semestrally and annually, respectively;
b. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer be required
to update the Ledger Cards and Stock and Property Cards in order
that the reconciliation of the physical count against book balances can
be facilitated; and
c. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile the
accounting and property records, and make the necessary adjustments
so that the accounts can be fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management
a. verify that projects to be funded by the 20% DF, and subsequent
disbursements made thereof, conform to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1
dated February 22, 2017 in order that the desired socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes geared mainly
towards the creation of jobs, promotion of economic growth and
eradication of poverty, as envisioned by the national government, may
be achieved;
b. immediately stop from utilizing the 20% DF for projects, programs and
activities which are not properly chargeable thereto; and
c. advise the MPDC Officer to prepare a revised AIP for the 20% DF for
the realignment of those unobligated appropriations which were not
properly chargeable thereto.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to furnish the Auditor copies of the contract of the foregoing projects
together with the supporting documents for review of the latter. Moreover,
Management is required to adhere strictly to the provisions of COA Circular No.
2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to furnish the Auditor the lacking documentary requirements and
that, henceforth, all transactions be supported with the complete documentary
requirements prior to disbursement and that the provisions of the Revised IRR of
RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be strictly adhered
to.

We recommended that the Management
a. render proper accounting of the difference noted from the purchased
and distributed food items;
b. install sound internal control mechanism in the repacking of goods to
monitor the release of items for repacking and account the packed
relief items to ensure that these are safeguarded from possible theft,
loss and wastage;
c. require the MDRRMO or property officer to prepare reports on the
inventory and monitoring of all procured and donated supplies,
materials, equipment, and relief goods in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014; and
d. direct the Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies,
medicines, and relief goods for distribution in accordance with the
aforementioned COA Circular.

We recommended that:
a. The MSWDO submit a written explanation on the difference between
the number of target beneficiaries (11,988) and the actual beneficiaries
found on the master list (11,928) and on the lapses and deficiencies
enumerated in this observation;
b. The amount received by the 31 job order personnel of the LGU who
appeared in the master list of paid beneficiaries be refunded;
c. Submit all the lacking photocopies of IDs of the beneficiaries signing
the payroll; and
d. Management to establish a system in the distribution of SAP and
similar cash assistance programs wherein the guidelines issued by the
DSWD are followed and that proper validation and appropriate
profiling of beneficiaries are performed before disbursement.

We recommended that Management submit the activity design related to
the procurement activity, copies of the daily monitoring sheets of the PUMs, and
copies of DTRs of LGU personnel provided with meals in order to validate the
usage thereof. Moreover, we recommended that the end user and the BAC adhere
strictly to Section 7.3.2 (f) of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of procuring items
through special cash advances, and direct the BAC to follow the rules and
regulations on procurement as provided under RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR,
and the GPPB to obtain the most advantageous price to the government.
Moreover, cash advances, other than those related to travel, should only be
granted to employees who are bonded, and for those who were not, only grant up
to an amount of ₱5,000.00.

We recommended that, henceforth, elected officials such as the Municipal
Mayor, shall be allowed to draw cash advances solely for their traveling expenses.
We also recommended that the rules and regulations on the granting and
utilization of cash advance be strictly followed in accordance with COA Circular
No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards
Committee to submit justification to prove reasonableness for the procurement of
the aforecited items above the suggested price of the DOH. Moreover, procure
medical supplies at reasonable prices guided by the SRP set by appropriate
government agencies to ensure procurement that is most advantageous to the
government.

We recommended that Management require the recipient barangays to
liquidate the funds by submitting the documentary requirements prescribed
under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, and
henceforth, comply with the documentary requirements on fund transfers.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Budget Office to thoroughly evaluate and review the
submitted PPMPs in terms of amount and coherence with the proposed
budget of the LGU;
b. Require the BAC through the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the
PPMPs into a comprehensive, workable, and realistic APP, which will
serve as basis for procurement, in accordance to the applicable
provisions of RA No. 9184 using the prescribed format under GPPB
Circular No. 07-2015; and
c. Submit the duly approved copy of the APP to the GPPB in accordance
with AO No. 46, series of 2015, Executive Order (EO) No. 662, series of
2007, as amended, and standards prescribed by the GPPB.

We recommended that Management instruct the LDRRM Officer to
include PPAs chargeable against the prior years unexpended LDRRMF in the
annual LDRRMFIP of the current year pursuant to NDRRMC-DILG-DBM JMC
No. 2013-1 and the same be prepared in adherence to the format prescribed under
Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management
a. immediately stop from utilizing the LDRRMF for PPAs which are not
properly chargeable to the Fund;
b. henceforth, the PPAs to be included in the annual LDRRMF Investment
Plan should conform to the PPAs enumerated in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG
JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; and
c. the amount of ₱118,948.00 representing a portion of the LDRRMF
appropriations spent expenses which are not properly chargeable thereto,
should be refunded from the appropriations of the GF Proper.

We recommended and the LDRRM Officer agreed to prepare the Monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF duly reconciled with the accounting
records and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, and submit the report
to the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor desist from granting additional cash advances to concerned accountable officers.

projects at its total cost and recognize liability for any unpaid account. We
further recommended that she draw the following entry in the General Fund
to correct the accounts of the municipality:
Account Debit Credit
1 07 03 010 Road Networks ₱3,772,381.20
1 07 10 020 Construction-in-Progress – 650,685.14 ₱3,772,381.20
Infrastructure Assets
2 01 01 010 Accounts Payable 650,685.14

We recommended that municipal officials endeavour to avoid the
incurrence of unnecessary expenditures and ensure that only essential items
necessary in the LGU’s operations are purchased in order to safeguard the
municipality’s resources.

We recommended that the municipality limit the payment of hazard
allowance only to days an employee is actually exposed to occupational risks
due to the type of cases/clientele they actually handled as enumerated under
Section 16.1.1 of the IRR of R.A. 9433, as well as during assignments to
remote and depressed areas, strife-torn or embattled areas, distressed or
isolated stations, mental hospitals, leprosaria, areas declared under a state of
calamity or emergency which expose them to great danger, and volcanic
activity/eruption. We further recommended that claims for hazard
allowance be supported with proof of exposure to risk for each day claimed.

We recommended that the municipality endeavor to fast-track the
implementation of development projects under the 20% DF in order that the
benefits can be immediately enjoyed by target beneficiaries as well as ensure
that these are completed at the time they are still relevant or needed. We
further recommended that the 20% DF be used to finance only those projects
which are among the allowable uses thereof in conformity with existing
guidelines. Lastly, we recommended that appropriate steps be taken to
correct the books for the erroneous recognition of capital assets for funds
transferred to the barangays amounting to ₱900,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist initiate the repair
of the water reservoir/tank in order that it can supply the required quantity
of water to operate all ten water boxes of the hatchery and thus attain their
intended purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain special
accounts for the Terminal Complex and Spring Resort in order that
management can be informed of the profitability or non-profitability of their
operations and can thus decide accordingly.

We recommended that the municipality comply with the guidelines
for the payment of Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situations by releasing
cash assistance to individuals who are currently in need so that the intended
purpose of the fund will be achieved.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider limiting or reducing the number of job order workers to be hired to what is necessary for the implementation of projects such that the wages of these laborers will be correctly charged to the projects being implemented.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor reduced the job order workers to 300 and justified that since the municipality is a first class municipality, they needed job order workers for the operation of its economic enterprises.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management monitor the implementation of the Evacuation and Multi-purpose Center project and impose sanctions and penalties, if warranted, for the delay in the completion thereof pursuant to the pertinent provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Appropriated funds for the Evacuation and Multi-purpose Center.
observations.reason: The project was implemented by the Kalahi-CIDDS whose funds were not enough for the programmed works.

observations.recommendation: We further recommend that henceforth, municipal officials who issue clearance, authorize and participate in the release of final amounts due officials who retire, die, separate or transfer see to it that unliquidated cash advances are deducted from their claims.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason: Some cash advances were already fully settled. However, there were still unsettled cash advances of deceased and retired accountable officers which are deemed to be recoverable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local School Board observe the limitations on the use of SEF especially in the preparation of the budget and to limit charges thereto to purposes allowed under Section 272 of the Local Government Code and DECS –DBM –DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 1998.
observations.managementAction: The municipality followed the latest SEF guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the allocation of funds, the Local Council for the Protection of Children prioritize projects and activities geared towards the protection of children and the prevention of juvenile delinquency, and to endeavor to implement these projects within the year they are programmed.
observations.managementAction: The municipality implemented the projects and payment of LCPC.
observations.reason: The LCPC fund was used for the honoraria of day care workers which is not aligned with the guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances and issue demand letters to officers and employees who fail to liquidate within the reglementary period, otherwise, cause the withholding of the salaries of accountable officers concerned pursuant to Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Henceforth, adhere to the provision of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: On-going salary deduction is done by the accounting office.
observations.reason: Cash advances aged more than one year were still unliquidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management observe the limitation in the appropriation for confidential and intelligence fund as provided under COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor has appropriated ₱300,000.00 for confidential and intelligence fund for CY 2017 which is 30% of their appropriation for Peace and Order of ₱1,000,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Chairperson, GAD Focal Point System ensure that GAD funds will be utilized to address gender issues as indicated in the GAD Plan and ensure that the direct beneficiaries of the projects and programs match the performance targets. We further recommended that a permanent employee of the municipality be appointed as the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System.
observations.managementAction: The performance targets as indicated in the 2013 GAD Plan had been matched with the direct beneficiaries. A permanent employee of the municipality has been designated as GFP.

As of December 31, 2017, the municipality, through the Municipal Treasurer had an ongoing conduct of physical count of its movable property, plant and equipment amounting to ₱47,995,703.64 or 8.6% of total assets.
observations.reason: However, her records were not yet reconciled with the accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management observe the applicable regulations on the grant of fringe benefits and refund the overpayment of the Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2014.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor instructed the department heads to refund the PEI and to henceforth observe the applicable regulations on the grant of any fringe benefits.
observations.reason: On-going salary deduction is done by the accounting office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the deposits of the local government of Mabinay with the FCB be transferred to the authorized government depository bank and to ensure that government funds are properly safeguarded against loss or wastage.
observations.managementAction: The account with FCB was closed in June 2017.

observations.recommendation: Require the officials and employees concerned to refund in full the amount received by them, as they are in violation of the aforementioned laws and regulations. Moreover, subsequent claims for increases in RATA shall also be disallowed in audit until the requirements for the PS cap have been met. Direct the department heads and all officials concerned to refrain collecting the proposed RATA increases in 2010. Direct also the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to be vigilant and not to certify disbursements which go beyond the provisions of the law.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials refunded the RATA while ongoing salary deduction is done by the accounting office.
observations.reason: Some officials refunded the RATA while ongoing salary deduction was effected to the others.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management observe the limitation in the appropriation for confidential and intelligence fund as provided under COA- DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.reason: Appropriation still above the allowable limit.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management exercise prudence in spending government funds and refrain from incurring extravagant expenditures in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-003.
observations.reason: The municipality still held the ELA in the city of Bacolod.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management submit a terminal report on output in the conduct of the Mid-Year Evaluation of the ELA, particularly that affecting the DepEd and henceforth, ensure that charges to the Special Education Fund are in consonance with Section 272 of RA 7160.
observations.reason: The terminal report was not yet submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exercise prudence in the disbursement of funds and avail of the unlimited call and texts promos of Globe and Smart networks to cut on telephone-mobile expenses.
observations.reason: The municipality had not discussed the issue to the department heads.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Chairperson, GAD Focal Point System to address identified gender issues and ensure that the direct beneficiaries of the projects and programs match the performance targets. We further recommended that a permanent employee of the municipality be appointed as the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System.
observations.reason: There were still GAD activities that did not address the identified gender issues and performance targets.

observations.recommendation: Require the Local Chief Executive to refund the questioned financial assistance and comply with DBM SARO No.-BMB-G-13-T000000849 which limits the grant of financial assistance to residents of Mabinay only.
observations.reason: The audit team is still waiting for the justification of the recipient that she is a resident of Mabinay, Negros Oriental.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the deposits of the local government of Mabinay with the FCB be transferred to the authorized government depository bank to ensure that government funds are properly safeguarded against loss or wastage.
observations.reason: The municipality still has an account with FCB.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management consider the disposal of its unserviceable properties; request the appraisal committee to make an appraisal of each item in the Inventory Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP); have these items inspected and appraised by the Regional Technical Information Services, COA Region VII; and undertake the disposal following procedures and guidelines under Sec. 502-505 of the GAAM, Vol. I. Require the supply office and the accountant to drop the amounts of the unserviceable property from their books after disposal and the accountant to record the proceeds of the auction sale, if any.
observations.reason: The municipality has not yet created an appraisal and disposal committee.

observations.recommendation: Require the officials and employees concerned to refund in full the amount received by them, as they are in violation of the aforementioned laws and regulations. Direct the department heads and all officials concerned to refrain from collecting proposed RATA increases in 2010. Direct also the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to be vigilant and not to certify disbursements which go beyond the provisions of the law.
observations.reason: The municipality had not effected refund; persons liable remain to be pursued.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Treasurer as head of the Market committee to minimize expenditures for the Market Operations and take steps to cut expenditures until such time that the income therefrom is sufficient to cover said expenditures. Require the Municipal Treasurer to effect an inter-fund transfer from the General Fund Proper to the Market Operation in the amount of P8,693,138.76.
observations.reason: The municipality had not effected the inter-fund transfer from the General Fund to the Market Operations.

observations.recommendation: Require the Assessor, as well as, the Municipal Treasurer to convene the Provincial Appraisal Committee to immediately act in the determination of the value of the unrecorded lots. Also direct them to process the titling of the lots owned by the municipality and for the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling thereof. Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to review the land account and record only in the books of accounts those lots that are owned by the municipality. The value of the lots which will be determined by the Provincial Appraisal Committee shall also be recorded in the books of accounts to avoid understatement of assets.
observations.reason: The Assessor and Treasurer have no records of the land owned by the municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider limiting or
reducing the number of job order workers to be hired to what is necessary
for the implementation of projects such that the wages of these laborers will
be correctly charged to the projects being implemented.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee be required
to submit to the auditor within the reglementary period of five (5) working
days from perfection, two (2) sets of the contract and all supporting
documents for the auditorial and technical review thereof pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that management monitor the implementation of
the project and impose sanctions and penalties, if warranted, for the delay in
the completion thereof pursuant to the pertinent provisions of the Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Administrator – Designate
discharge her duties within the limitations set forth under her designation
order as well as Section 39 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992.

We recommended the imposition of sanctions as provided under
Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 specifically the filing of
malversation charges and appropriate administrative proceedings against

on the use of the Special Education Fund especially in the preparation of the

for the Protection of Children prioritize projects and activities geared
towards the protection of children and the prevention of juvenile
delinquency, and to endeavor to implement these projects within the year

We recommended that management ensure the implementation of the
projects programmed under the 20% Development Fund so that the socio-
economic benefits that could be derived therefrom can be enjoyed by the

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances and issue demand letters to officers and employees who fail to liquidate within the reglementary period, otherwise, cause the withholding of the salaries of accountable officers concerned pursuant to Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Henceforth, adhere to the provision of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the LDRRMO to observe the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 in the usage of the LDRRMF to ensure proper utilization of the fund.
observations.managementAction: The municipality had observed the provisions of law in the usage of the LDRRMF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly recorded transactions in the books of accounts in accordance with the PPSAS and Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards. We further recommended that she draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to take up the following entries: Account Title Debit Credit (details in report).
observations.managementAction: The municipality properly recorded transactions in the books of accounts in accordance with the PPSAS and adjusted the books as recommended.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor determine the person responsible for the renewal of the motor vehicle and require him/her to refund immediately the corresponding penalty erroneously charged to government funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer explained that the penalties were due to the delayed acceptance of the LTO of their payment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality comply with the guidelines for the payment of Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situations by releasing cash assistance directly to the service provider concerned as required under DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 02, series of 2014.
observations.managementAction: Cash assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation were limited only to ₱3,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended: a. Instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to make representations with the various depository banks to obtain the necessary evidence/documentation to support the correction of book and bank errors; b. Require the Accounting Office to give priority to and intensify the review and analysis of the existing reconciling items and adjust those which are clearly identified; and c. Henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare the BRS of each bank account and immediately prepare adjusting entries for book reconciling items.
observations.reason: Bank Reconciliation Statement no longer showed long outstanding reconciling items.

observations.recommendation: Require the officials and employees concerned to complete the physical count of the properties and to undertake the said activity annually; require the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the count with the accounting records and make the necessary adjustments so that the accounts can be fairly presented in the financial statements; update the issuances of AREs and ensure that all movable assets are covered; require the Property Officer to prepare individual ARE for every property issued and renew the records every three (3) years thereafter pursuant to Section 42 of the NGAS manual.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2016, the Treasurer and OIC - Municipal Accountant had an on-going conduct of physical count of its movable property, plant and equipment amounting to ₱39,553,607.86 or 8.03% of total assets.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer and OIC - Municipal Accountant still have to reconcile the accounting and property records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive refund immediately the amount received by them, as they are in violation of the aforementioned laws and regulations. Moreover, subsequent claims for increases in RATA shall also be disallowed in audit until the requirements for the PS cap have been met.
observations.managementAction: Only some personnel refunded the PEI through payroll deduction.
observations.reason: Only the department heads refunded the PEI; further refunds pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended: a. Ensure that meals and snacks served during meetings, seminars and for the entertainment of guests or visitors shall be properly charged against the representation allowance/s of the concerned LGU officials granted the same; b. Require the Municipal Accountant to check the propriety and validity of the claims for catering services for seminars, meetings and special occasions and not to pass payment claims for catering services unless these are duly supported with proper and complete documentation and that the funds have been duly appropriated for the purpose; c. Stop the practice of serving meals and snacks during ordinary staff meetings or conferences in order to prevent the incurrence of irregular and unnecessary expenditures and maximize the use of the limited resources of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: The municipality stopped the serving of meals and snacks during meetings and entertainment of guests; the Municipal Accountant checks claims.
